channel.title: books on Musings on the modern world

item.title: Press Reset - Review
item.description:
Press Reset by video game journalist Jason Schreier details how unstable development is with its always looming layoffs, office moves across the country and project cancellations.

The book starts in the late 80s and early 90s with famous video game designer Warren Specter, detailing his move across various publishers, trying to make games he is passionate about but which never quite hit the nerve of a mass audience. From there on, the book moves forward through the decades, covering the experiences of dozens of industry figures. The interesting thing with the book’s storytelling is that you start to realise how small the industry is. Although Schreier mostly focuses on individual projects in each chapter, employee and company names repeat throughout and start to ring a bell based on earlier chapters. It becomes obvious how often employees have to jump from company to company, often across the entire American continent, uprooting their lives only for having to be laid off yet again and having to move another time.

Reading this book makes you realise how difficult it must be to create a game: Usually, multiple studios are working on the same title, often spread across different cities or even continents. Many of these games don’t even see the light of day because suddenly the publisher might decide that the project is not good enough. And if a game comes out, it is not always a given that this project is a success: not every title can be an Uncharted - or whatever your favourite game is. In fact, many of the titles mentioned in the book were unknown to me or were reported by Schreier to be a failure in some way or form. This stands completely at odds with the many sacrifices creators make as we learn throughout the book. Workers in the industry give their all, both in terms of life time and life quality, just to fulfil their dream of making games.

If you are interested too then you might start reading this article on the outlet Polygon which details the numerous ways Bioshock Infinite was a sheer catastrophe to work on, despite the overall fidelity of the final product. This article is an excerpt from Schreier’s Press Reset and I found it so fascinating that I just had to get the entire book. And I don’t regret it, I haven’t read a book which this much vigor in years. Highly recommended.

item.title: Little Fires Everywhere - Book Review
item.description:
It is rare that a book comes along which makes me think so much about its themes and subjects. In that way, “Little Fires Everywhere” could be on track of becoming a modern classic.

It is one of those books where the characters feel completely life-like. For a novel grounded in the reality of the 1990s in the US, the plot is quite exciting and there are plenty of revelations and events which keep you on your toe. The story discusses the tensions between two different families and their opposing ideas of what a good life means. And tensions do run high towards the end of the book, which culminates in an explosive finale. But it bothered me a bit that there are so many conflicts opened up but few of them are actually followed through to their fullest. Many conflicts brood between the lines and remain unspoken. This seems like some missed opportunity.

It is a very feminine story, which I found highly refreshing - there aren’t a lot of male characters in this book, and if so these are only side characters. The only male person with an actual personality is teenage son Moody; but we know barely anything about all other male characters; certainly not how they feel, only what they do. Even son Trip, whose role is reasonably prominent, presents himself solely as a good-looking sportsman and this is also pretty much the only reason why teenage girl Pearl desires him. Some might argue that this is only a fair reversal of the usual tropes for literature romances; others might say it’s sexism in reverse. Be that as it may.

Many reviewers suggest that “Little Fires Everywhere” talks a lot about race but I have to vehemently disagree with this. One character in this book is Asian and one minor side character is black - how would that make this book explore race? A far bigger problem is that many of these critics and also most of the characters in the book - heck, even the narrator! - seem to confuse race with culture. What, so a person of Asian race needs to know about Chinese culture when they grow up in America? Does a black Frenchman have to know about “the customs of their people”? I find it deplorable that the issue of race relations has to be projected onto every work of art even if it’s completely unrelated. In the case of “Little Fires Everywhere”, I’m sure the author enjoys this line of thought as it makes for great publicity, but I absolutely fail to see how this book discusses or even just depicts the topic of race.
